content.text:
{For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.}{Ephesians 2:10}
 {Mathematics is not about numbers, equations, computations, or algorithms: it is about understanding.}{— William Paul Thurston}

Matrices appear throughout modern applications. In this section, we 
explore several contexts where matrix algebra provides essential tools.

Every image on your screen is manipulated using matrix operations. 
Transformations such as rotations, scaling, and translations are 
represented as matrices.

content.title: 2D transformations

content.text:
To combine transformations, multiply the matrices. If you first rotate 
by $\theta$, then scale, the combined transformation is $S \cdot R_\theta$ 
(applied right-to-left).

content.title: Stochastic Matrix
content.content:
A **stochastic matrix** (or 
**transition matrix**) is a square matrix $P$ where:

- All entries are nonnegative.

- Each column sums to 1.

content.text: Stochastic matrices model systems that evolve probabilistically.

content.title: Weather model
content.problem:
A simple weather model has two states: Sunny (S) and Rainy (R). The 
transition probabilities are:

- If today is sunny, there is a 70\% chance tomorrow is sunny.

- If today is rainy, there is a 40\% chance tomorrow is sunny.

The transition matrix is:
\[
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Column 1 gives probabilities if today is sunny; column 2 if rainy.

If today we have 60\% chance of sun ($\vect{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$), 
then tomorrow:
\[
\vect{x}_1 = P\vect{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.58 \\ 0.42 \end{bmatrix}
\]

After $n$ days: $\vect{x}_n = P^n \vect{x}_0$.

content.text: Matrices have been used for encryption since World War I.

content.title: Hill cipher
content.problem:
The **Hill cipher** encrypts messages using matrix multiplication. 
Convert letters to numbers (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25), group into vectors, 
and multiply by an invertible "key" matrix modulo 26.

With key $K = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$ and message 
"HI" (H=7, I=8):
\[
K\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 3 \cdot 7 + 3 \cdot 8 \\ 2 \cdot 7 + 5 \cdot 8 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 45 \\ 54 \end{bmatrix}
\equiv \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \pmod{26}
\]
This gives "TC." To decrypt, multiply by $K\inv$ (mod 26).

content.title: Leslie matrix
content.problem:
A **Leslie matrix** models population dynamics. 
Consider a species with three age classes. The first row contains 
birth rates; subdiagonal entries are survival rates:
\[
L = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.6 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\]

If the current population is 
$\vect{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 80 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}$ (young, 
middle, old), then next year:
\[
\vect{x}_1 = L\vect{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 + 160 + 25 \\ 50 + 0 + 0 \\ 0 + 48 + 0 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 185 \\ 50 \\ 48 \end{bmatrix}
\]

content.text:
The **adjacency matrix** of a graph has $a_{ij} = 1$ if there is 
an edge from node $i$ to node $j$, and $0$ otherwise.

content.title: Counting paths
content.problem:
The entry $(A^k)_{ij}$ counts the number of paths of length $k$ from 
node $i$ to node $j$. This result, while surprising, follows from the 
definition of matrix multiplication.

marginNotes.text:
A word fitly spoken is like apples 
of gold in a setting of silver.

marginNotes.author: Claude Shannon (1916--2001)
marginNotes.text:
The fundamental problem of 
communication is that of reproducing at one point either exactly or 
approximately a message selected at another point.
